walkthrough.title: Find a Flat Surface
walkthrough.text: First things first — Animal Upon Animal is a stacking game, so you need a nice flat, even surface. A wobbly table is your worst enemy here. Clear off the play area and make sure everyone can comfortably reach the center of the table. No bumping elbows allowed when there is a precarious animal pyramid at stake.

walkthrough.title: Unpack the Animals
walkthrough.text: Open the box and take out all 29 wooden animals and the one special die. You will see a wonderful menagerie of chunky wooden creatures: penguins, hedgehogs, snakes, toucans, sheep, monkeys, lizards, and one big crocodile. Take a moment to admire them — they are beautifully crafted and each one has a unique shape that matters for stacking.

walkthrough.title: Place the Crocodile
walkthrough.text: Find the crocodile. It is the largest animal and has a long, flat body — you cannot miss it. Place the crocodile in the center of the table where everyone can reach it. The crocodile is the foundation of your animal pyramid. All stacking starts here.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Remaining Animals
walkthrough.text: Set the crocodile aside (it is already on the table) and look at the remaining 28 animals. There are seven different animal types, and four copies of each. The types are: penguin, hedgehog, snake, toucan, sheep, monkey, and lizard (collar lizard). Each animal has a distinct shape that will challenge your stacking skills in different ways.

walkthrough.title: Deal Animals to Players
walkthrough.text: Each player selects seven different animals and places them in front of themselves. This is your personal supply — or as the rulebook calls it, your 'provision.' Try to take one of each animal type so everyone has a nice variety. With 2 or 3 players, you will have leftover animals. Just put those back in the box — they will not be used this game.

walkthrough.title: Grab the Die
walkthrough.text: Find the special wooden die with symbols on it. This is not a normal numbered die — it has five different symbols: one dot, two dots, a crocodile, a hand, and a question mark. Each symbol tells you what to do on your turn. Keep the die near the center of the table where the active player can easily grab it.

walkthrough.title: Determine the First Player
walkthrough.text: Here is the fun part. According to the official rules, whoever can balance on one foot like a flamingo for the longest time gets to start the game. Yes, really! If nobody wants to do the flamingo test, or you cannot agree on a winner, the youngest player rolls the die first. Play proceeds clockwise from the starting player.

walkthrough.title: You Are Ready to Play!
walkthrough.text: That is the entire setup. You have a crocodile in the center of the table, each player has seven animals in front of them, and the die is ready to roll. The goal is simple: be the first player to get rid of all your animals by stacking them onto the pyramid. Let's learn how a turn works.

summary.title: You Are Ready to Play!
bullets: Crocodile in center, 7 animals per player, die ready
Goal: be first to get rid of all your animals

walkthrough.title: On Your Turn: Roll the Die
walkthrough.text: Every turn starts the same way. You roll the special die and check which symbol came up. The symbol tells you exactly what you need to do this turn. There are five possible results, and each one creates a different challenge. Let's go through all of them.

walkthrough.title: Die Result: One Dot
walkthrough.text: If you roll one dot, take one animal from your supply and carefully place it onto the animal pyramid using only one hand. You can put it anywhere on the pyramid — on top of existing animals, wedged between them, balanced on an edge — anywhere, as long as it ends up on the pyramid. The one-hand rule is important: no steadying the pyramid with your other hand!

walkthrough.title: Die Result: Two Dots
walkthrough.text: If you roll two dots, you must place two animals from your supply onto the pyramid, one at a time, using only one hand. This is great because you get to offload two animals in one turn — but it is also riskier because two placements means two chances for things to go wrong. Place them carefully, one after the other.

walkthrough.title: Die Result: The Crocodile
walkthrough.text: If you roll the crocodile symbol, you get to expand the base of the pyramid. Take one animal from your supply and place it next to the crocodile's mouth or tail so that the two pieces are touching. This makes the foundation wider, giving everyone more surface area to stack on in future turns. Each time anyone rolls the crocodile, they can place their animal next to any animal already touching the crocodile, expanding the base even further.

walkthrough.title: Die Result: The Hand
walkthrough.text: If you roll the hand, you get to play a little trick. Choose any one animal from your supply and hand it to another player. That player must now stack your animal onto the pyramid on their next action. This is a great way to offload a tricky animal — like the hedgehog or the snake — onto someone else. Choose wisely!

walkthrough.title: Die Result: The Question Mark
walkthrough.text: If you roll the question mark, the other players get to choose which of your animals you must place on the pyramid. They will look at your remaining animals and pick the one they think will be hardest for you to stack. You still place it yourself, but they choose which one. Handle with care — your opponents will definitely pick your most awkward piece!

walkthrough.title: What Happens When Animals Fall
walkthrough.text: Stacking is tricky, and animals will tumble. Here is what happens when they do. If one or two animals fall off the pyramid during your stacking attempt, you must take those fallen animals and add them to your personal supply. That is a setback — you just got more animals to get rid of. If more than two animals fall, you only take two of the fallen animals. The rest go back into the game box and are removed from the game entirely.

walkthrough.title: Total Pyramid Collapse
walkthrough.text: If the entire pyramid collapses, the stacking player takes two animals from the wreckage. The crocodile goes back to the center of the table and all remaining fallen animals are returned to the box. You rebuild from scratch with just the crocodile. Also note: if animals fall off on their own without anyone touching the pyramid, all the fallen animals simply go back in the box. Nobody gets penalized for gravity being unfair.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The game ends immediately when a player places their last animal. That player wins and is crowned the best animal stacker of the day! Since the goal is to get rid of all your animals, every die roll is a chance to shed one or two pieces from your supply. Rolling well, stacking carefully, and avoiding collapses is the path to victory.

walkthrough.title: Variation for Experts
walkthrough.text: Once your group has mastered the basic game, try the expert rules for an extra challenge. First, if you have fewer than four players, distribute all remaining animals evenly between players instead of boxing them. Second, when animals fall, the stacking player takes up to five animals instead of two. Third, when a player is down to just one animal, they skip the die roll entirely and must immediately place that last animal on the pyramid. These rules make the game tighter and more intense.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Jungle
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a few scripted turns with three players — Alice, Bob, and Carol. Each will face different die results and stacking challenges so you can see how the game flows. Follow along at your own table if you like. Ready? Let's stack some animals!

walkthrough.title: The Starting Pyramid
walkthrough.text: The crocodile is sitting flat in the center of the table. Each player has seven animals in front of them. Alice has the first turn because she won the flamingo balance contest. The die is in the center, waiting to be rolled.

walkthrough.title: Alice's Turn: One Dot
walkthrough.text: Alice rolls the die and gets one dot. She picks up her sheep — it has a nice flat back, perfect for the first placement. Using only one hand, she carefully places the sheep on the crocodile's back. It sits there perfectly. Solid start! Alice now has six animals left in her supply.

walkthrough.title: Why the Sheep Was a Smart Choice
walkthrough.text: Alice chose the sheep because it has a broad, flat back that other animals can be stacked on later. Early in the game, placing animals with flat surfaces is a great idea because they create stable platforms for everyone. The sheep is one of the friendliest animals to stack early.

walkthrough.title: Bob's Turn: The Crocodile Symbol
walkthrough.text: Bob rolls the die and gets the crocodile symbol. This means he places one of his animals next to the crocodile, expanding the base. Bob takes his lizard and carefully sets it next to the crocodile's tail so the two pieces are touching. Now the foundation is wider — there is more room for stacking in future turns. Bob has six animals left.

walkthrough.title: Carol's Turn: Two Dots
walkthrough.text: Carol rolls two dots — she gets to place two animals! She picks her toucan first and balances it on the sheep that Alice placed. Then she takes her monkey and carefully sets it next to the toucan on the crocodile's back. Both animals stay put. Carol is down to five animals already. Nice turn!

walkthrough.title: Alice's Second Turn: The Hand
walkthrough.text: Alice rolls the hand symbol. She grins and looks at her animals. She picks up her snake — the long, squiggly, difficult-to-balance snake — and hands it to Bob. Now Bob has to stack Alice's snake onto the pyramid. Bob groans. The snake is notoriously tricky because of its curved shape. Alice still has five animals left (she got rid of the snake from her supply).

walkthrough.title: Bob Stacks Alice's Snake
walkthrough.text: Bob has to place Alice's snake on the pyramid. He thinks carefully and decides to drape it across the toucan and the sheep, letting it curve naturally over both animals. It wobbles for a moment... and holds! The pyramid is getting taller and more precarious. Good nerve, Bob.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Using Two Hands
walkthrough.text: Wait — did you notice Bob almost reached out with his left hand to steady the snake while placing it with his right? That is a very common mistake. Remember: you must use only one hand when placing animals. No steadying the pyramid, no holding animals in place while you adjust. One hand only! If your group is playing casually with young kids, you might relax this rule, but officially it is one hand.

walkthrough.title: Carol's Second Turn: The Question Mark
walkthrough.text: Carol rolls the question mark. Alice and Bob look at Carol's remaining animals and confer. They see she still has the hedgehog — that round, spiky little ball. They pick the hedgehog with evil grins. Carol has to place the hedgehog on the increasingly wobbly pyramid. She carefully balances it on the monkey's back... and it stays! Carol breathes a sigh of relief. Four animals left.

walkthrough.title: Alice's Third Turn: Disaster Strikes
walkthrough.text: Alice rolls one dot and decides to place her penguin on top of the growing pyramid. She reaches over and sets the penguin on top of the snake, but the penguin tips sideways and knocks the hedgehog and the toucan off the pyramid. Two animals have fallen! According to the rules, Alice must take those two fallen animals into her supply. She went from five animals down to... well, still five (she placed one but took two back, net minus one placed plus two gained). Ouch.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Placing on Unstable Spots
walkthrough.text: Alice's mistake was placing the penguin on top of the snake — a curved, unstable surface. The lesson here is to look for flat, stable areas before committing to a placement. The snake's body curves and wobbles, making it a terrible foundation. When possible, place animals on flat backs (like the sheep or crocodile) or wedge them between two stable contact points. Think about weight distribution!

walkthrough.title: Bob's Second Turn: One Dot
walkthrough.text: Bob rolls one dot. The pyramid has lost a couple of animals from Alice's collapse, so there is a bit more room now. Bob chooses his sheep and places it flat on the crocodile's back next to the snake. Stable and safe. Bob is playing conservatively after watching Alice's disaster. Six animals left for Bob.

walkthrough.title: Carol's Third Turn: One Dot
walkthrough.text: Carol rolls one dot. She places her snake across the two sheep on the pyramid, draping it like a bridge. The snake's curves let it hook onto both sheep nicely. Creative placement — and it worked! Carol is down to three animals. She is in the lead.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Knocking Over the Pyramid While Reaching
walkthrough.text: Careful! Bob just bumped the table reaching for the die. The pyramid wobbled but held. This is another common source of collapses — bumping the table, reaching across other players, or setting down your elbow too close to the action. Make sure the pyramid is in the center where nobody has to stretch too far. And keep your elbows in check!

walkthrough.title: The Race Heats Up
walkthrough.text: A few more turns pass. Carol is down to her last two animals. Alice still has five because of her earlier collapse. Bob has four. The pyramid is now a towering, wobbly mess of wooden creatures. Every placement is nerve-wracking. The table goes quiet as each player carefully studies the pyramid before committing to a spot.

walkthrough.title: Carol's Final Turns
walkthrough.text: Carol rolls one dot. She has two animals left — her penguin and her monkey. She picks the penguin and finds a tiny flat spot between two animals near the top. With a surgeon's steady hand, she sets it down. It holds! One animal left. On her next turn, Carol rolls one dot again. She places her monkey on the edge of the crocodile where there is still some room. It stays. Carol wins! She is the best animal stacker of the day!

walkthrough.title: Before You Stack
walkthrough.text:
You have seen all five die results in action, watched a collapse and its consequences, and picked up on three common mistakes: using two hands, placing on unstable surfaces, and bumping the table. You are ready to play a full game on your own.

First — check out General Tips for stacking techniques and strategy.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to each animal piece and die face.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun stacking!

Reference the Appendix for animal pieces and die faces

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, here are the biggest mistakes new players make. First, using two hands — it is so instinctive to steady the pyramid, but the rules say one hand only. Second, placing animals on curved or wobbly surfaces when flat ones are available. Third, rushing your placement instead of studying the pyramid first. Fourth, bumping the table or reaching too aggressively. And fifth, forgetting that when you roll the hand, you are giving away one of YOUR animals — that is a good thing! Many new players forget that the hand symbol helps you get rid of an animal. Avoid these traps and you will be stacking like a pro.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Starting Placements Wisely
walkthrough.text: Early in the game, the crocodile has plenty of room. Use this opportunity to place animals with flat backs — like the sheep — as your first pieces. These create stable platforms for later stacking. Avoid placing round or awkward animals early, because they make the pyramid unstable for everyone, including you. Building a solid foundation benefits all players, but it benefits you most because you chose where to put it.

walkthrough.title: Study the Pyramid Before You Touch It
walkthrough.text: Take a moment to look at the pyramid from different angles before you pick up an animal. Where are the flat spots? Which animals are wobbling? Where is the center of gravity? The best stackers plan their placement before they pick up their piece. Once you are holding an animal over the pyramid with one hand, it is much harder to think clearly. Plan first, then execute.

walkthrough.title: Use the Crocodile Roll to Your Advantage
walkthrough.text: Rolling the crocodile symbol is secretly one of the best results. You get to place an animal at the base level — no risk of knocking anything over — and you expand the foundation. Think of it as a free, safe placement. When you place at the base, put your animal where it creates a good stacking surface for your future turns, not for your opponents.

walkthrough.title: The Hand Symbol Is Your Friend
walkthrough.text: When you roll the hand, you get to give one of your animals to another player. This is one of the best rolls in the game because you reduce your supply without any stacking risk. Give away your most awkward animal — the one you dread stacking. Hedgehogs, snakes, and penguins are popular choices to dump on opponents. Bonus: the receiving player has to stack a piece they did not choose and may not want.

walkthrough.title: Know Your Animals
walkthrough.text: Each animal shape has strengths and weaknesses. The sheep has a flat back — great for building platforms. The snake is long and curved — tricky alone but useful as a bridge between two contact points. The toucan's beak creates an awkward weight distribution. The hedgehog is round and rolls easily. The penguin is tall and narrow. The monkey has a curled tail. The lizard is flat but irregular. Learn which animals are easy to place and which are nightmares, so you know what to hand off when you roll the hand.

walkthrough.title: Stay Calm Under Pressure
walkthrough.text: As the pyramid grows taller, tension builds. Everyone is watching. The table goes quiet. This is where shaky hands cause collapses. Take a breath before you place. Move slowly and deliberately. Do not let the audience pressure you into rushing. The game rewards patience and steady hands, not speed. If your hand is trembling, rest your wrist on the table edge for stability — just do not touch the pyramid with your off hand.

walkthrough.title: Play the Players, Not Just the Pyramid
walkthrough.text: Animal Upon Animal is not just about stacking — it is about the other players too. When you roll the hand, give your worst animal to the player who is closest to winning to slow them down. When you place at the base (crocodile roll), position your animal where it helps you on future turns. When opponents are stacking, watch their technique — you might spot a weakness you can exploit by handing them the hedgehog. The social game is just as important as the dexterity game.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Using two hands — one hand only for placement
Placing on curved surfaces when flat ones exist
Rushing instead of studying the pyramid first
Bumping the table or reaching too aggressively
Forgetting the hand symbol helps you shed an animal

summary.title: Choose Your Starting Placements Wisely
bullets: Place flat-backed animals (like sheep) early
Flat pieces create stable platforms for later stacking
Building a solid foundation benefits you most

Identify flat spots, wobbly areas, and the center of gravity
Plan your placement before picking up your animal

Expands the foundation for more stacking surface

summary.title: The Hand Symbol Is Your Friend

Dump your most awkward animal on an opponent
Popular handoffs: hedgehog, snake, penguin

summary.title: Know Your Animals

Snake: curved, tricky alone but useful as a bridge
Hedgehog: round, rolls easily — tough to stack

summary.title: Stay Calm Under Pressure

summary.title: Play the Players, Not Just the Pyramid
bullets: Hand your worst animals to the player closest to winning
Position base placements to benefit your future turns
Watch opponents' techniques for weaknesses to exploit

walkthrough.title: Master the Snake
walkthrough.text: The snake is the most divisive animal in the game. Beginners dread it; experts love it. The secret is to use the snake as a bridge. Instead of trying to balance it on a single point, drape it across two stable animals so both ends are supported. The snake's curve naturally hooks onto surfaces. Once you master bridge placement, the snake becomes one of the easiest animals to stack — and one of the hardest for opponents when you hand it to them.

walkthrough.title: Wedging Technique
walkthrough.text: Instead of placing animals on top of the pyramid, try wedging them between existing animals. Push a piece gently into a gap so it is supported on two or three sides. Wedged animals are extremely stable because they have multiple contact points, and they are nearly impossible to knock off without disturbing the entire structure. The lizard and the monkey are particularly good candidates for wedging due to their irregular shapes.

walkthrough.title: Weight Distribution Awareness
walkthrough.text: Every animal you place shifts the pyramid's center of gravity. Think about this before you commit. If the pyramid is leaning left, place your animal on the right side to counterbalance it. If the top is heavy, add to the lower levels or the base. Advanced players instinctively read the pyramid's balance and place animals to stabilize it — not because they are being nice, but because a stable pyramid is easier to stack on during their next turn.

walkthrough.title: Strategic Hand-Offs
walkthrough.text: The hand symbol is not just about getting rid of an awkward animal — it is a strategic weapon. Consider these factors: Who is closest to winning? Give them the hardest animal to slow them down. Who has shaky hands today? Give them the round hedgehog. Is the pyramid extremely wobbly? Hand off an animal to force an opponent to risk a collapse while you sit safely. Timing your hand-offs can swing the outcome of the game.

walkthrough.title: Base Expansion Strategy
walkthrough.text: When you roll the crocodile, think about where you expand the base. Placing at the crocodile's tail versus its mouth creates different geometry. Ideally, expand toward your seating position so you have an easier reach on future turns. Also consider which animals you have left — if you still have large, flat animals, create a base extension that supports those shapes. A well-placed base animal can be your personal stacking zone for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Order of Placement
walkthrough.text: When you roll two dots, the order you place your two animals matters enormously. Place the larger, flatter animal first to create a platform, then stack the smaller or trickier animal on top of it. If you reverse the order, the small animal gets buried under the large one and is harder to balance. Also, your first placement might shift the pyramid, so be ready to adjust your second placement on the fly.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Pyramid
walkthrough.text: Before every placement, do a quick structural assessment. Which animals are load-bearing? Which are just resting on top? Where are the stress points? If you can identify the load-bearing animals, you know where NOT to add weight (it could cause a cascade failure) and where it IS safe (surfaces that are already supporting weight tend to be locked in place). Advanced players see the pyramid as an engineering structure, not just a pile of toys.

walkthrough.title: The Expert Variant Edge
walkthrough.text: In the expert variant, you take up to five animals on a collapse instead of two, and your last animal is played without rolling the die. This changes the strategy significantly. First, avoid risky placements at all costs — the penalty is brutal. Second, get rid of your awkward animals early through hand-offs so your last animal is an easy one. Third, when an opponent is down to one animal, remember they will auto-place it without rolling. If the pyramid is wobbly, this can work against them. Keep the pyramid just stable enough for your own turns but precarious enough to threaten others.

summary.title: Master the Snake
bullets: Use the snake as a bridge — drape it across two stable animals
The curve naturally hooks onto surfaces
Beginners dread it, experts use it as one of the easiest placements

summary.title: Wedging Technique
bullets: Push animals into gaps between existing pieces
Multiple contact points = extremely stable
Lizard and monkey are great candidates for wedging

summary.title: Weight Distribution Awareness
bullets: Every placement shifts the center of gravity
Counterbalance: if leaning left, place on the right
Stabilizing the pyramid helps you on future turns

summary.title: Strategic Hand-Offs
bullets: Give hardest animals to the player closest to winning
Target players with shaky hands or who are rushing
Time hand-offs when the pyramid is most precarious

summary.title: Base Expansion Strategy
bullets: Expand the base toward your seating position for easier reach
Consider which animals you still need to place
A well-placed base animal becomes your personal stacking zone

summary.title: Order of Placement
bullets: On two-dot rolls: place larger/flatter animal first
First placement creates a platform for the second
Be ready to adjust your second placement on the fly

summary.title: Reading the Pyramid
bullets: Identify load-bearing animals vs. resting ones
Avoid adding weight to stress points

summary.title: The Expert Variant Edge
bullets: 5-animal penalty makes risky placements devastating
Get rid of awkward animals early via hand-offs
Keep pyramid stable enough for you, precarious for opponents

entries.definition: The large, flat base animal placed in the center of the table at the start of the game. All stacking begins on or around the crocodile. It is never part of any player's supply — it is always the foundation. When the crocodile die symbol is rolled, animals are placed next to the crocodile to expand the base.
A chunky animal with a broad, flat back. One of the easiest animals to stack because it creates a stable platform. Great choice for early placements. Four copies in the game.

A long, curved animal that is tricky for beginners but powerful for experts. Can be draped across two animals as a bridge. Its curves hook onto surfaces naturally. Often handed off to opponents via the hand symbol. Four copies in the game.

A tall, narrow animal that stands upright. Difficult to balance on uneven surfaces due to its small base. Better placed in low, stable positions rather than on top of the pyramid. Four copies in the game.
A small, round animal that tends to roll. One of the hardest animals to stack because it has very little flat surface area. A favorite animal to hand off to opponents. Four copies in the game.

A bird-shaped animal with a large beak that creates uneven weight distribution. The beak can be used as a hook on other animals but can also cause tipping if not carefully oriented. Four copies in the game.

An irregularly shaped animal with a curled tail. The tail can be useful for hooking onto other pieces but also makes flat stacking difficult. A good candidate for the wedging technique. Four copies in the game.

A flat, irregularly shaped animal. Relatively easy to place due to its thin profile. Can be slid into gaps or laid flat on surfaces. Works well as a base expansion piece when the crocodile is rolled. Four copies in the game.
Die result: place one animal from your supply onto the pyramid using one hand. The most common and straightforward die result.
Die result: place two animals from your supply onto the pyramid, one at a time, using one hand. Great for getting ahead but doubles the risk of a collapse.
Die result: place one animal from your supply next to the crocodile (or next to animals already touching the crocodile) to expand the base. A safe, ground-level placement with no stacking risk.
Die result: choose one of your animals and give it to another player. That player must stack it onto the pyramid. Reduces your supply with zero stacking risk for you.
Die result: the other players choose which of your animals you must place on the pyramid. They will typically pick your most awkward or difficult piece.
The rulebook's term for each player's personal supply of animals. You start with seven animals in your provision and try to empty it to win.
The growing structure of stacked animals in the center of the table. Starts as just the crocodile and grows as players add animals. The pyramid is the central play area of the game.

When one or more animals fall off the pyramid during a stacking attempt. A minor collapse (1-2 animals) means the stacking player takes the fallen animals. A major collapse (3+) means the player takes only 2 and the rest go to the box.
When the entire pyramid falls apart. The stacking player takes 2 animals, the crocodile returns to center, and all other fallen animals go back in the box. Play resumes from scratch.

All animal placements must be done using only one hand. You may not steady the pyramid, hold other animals, or use your second hand in any way during placement. This is the core dexterity challenge of the game.

When the crocodile symbol is rolled, the player places an animal at ground level next to the crocodile or next to animals already touching it. This widens the foundation and creates more surface area for stacking.

Optional rules for experienced players: distribute all remaining animals evenly (even with fewer than 4 players), take up to 5 animals on a collapse instead of 2, and skip the die roll when you have only one animal left (place it immediately).
A solo variant where you try to stack as many animals as possible onto the crocodile without the pyramid collapsing. A personal challenge to beat your own record.

The game ends immediately when a player places their last animal from their provision. That player is declared the best animal stacker of the day. There is no point scoring — just be the first to empty your supply.
